prompt: An AI feature in production starts returning occasional wrong answers. Why is it usually a mistake to page the on-call engineer the moment a single answer looks bad?
choices: Because model outputs are non-deterministic, so one bad sample is noise; you should alert on a rate or trend across many requests instead
Because wrong answers from an LLM are never a real problem worth investigating
Because on-call engineers cannot read model outputs
Because a single bad answer always means the whole provider is down
explanation: LLM outputs vary run to run, so one bad answer is expected noise, not a signal. You watch a rate — e.g. groundedness or thumbs-down across many requests — and alert when that trend breaks, which separates a real regression from normal variance.

prompt: Your AI assistant ships its prompt and model choice behind a feature flag. Mid-incident you discover a prompt change has degraded answers. What does the flag let you do?
choices: Permanently delete the bad prompt from version control
Roll back to the previous prompt/model by flipping a config value, with no emergency redeploy
Automatically rewrite the prompt to be correct
Charge the cost of the incident to the model provider
explanation: Shipping prompt and model behind a flag turns rollback into a config flip that takes seconds, instead of an emergency code deploy — exactly what you want when answers are regressing live.

prompt: Which of the following are 'golden signals' worth putting on the on-call dashboard for an LLM feature? (Select all that apply.)
choices: p95 time-to-first-token and total latency
Error / timeout rate, including provider 429s
Cost per request, trended over time
The exact GPU temperature inside the model provider's data center
explanation: Latency, error/timeout rate, and cost-per-request are signals you can see and act on. The provider's internal GPU temperature is neither visible to you nor actionable — it is not your operational signal.

prompt: The primary model provider starts returning timeouts and 429s, and your error rate spikes. Your feature has a secondary provider wired behind a provider abstraction. What is the appropriate operational response?
choices: Immediately rewrite the application to remove all AI features
Wait for the provider to recover and take no action
Fail over to the secondary provider via the flag and serve a graceful fallback for the brief overlap
Increase the retry count to infinite so requests eventually succeed
explanation: A pre-wired failover to the secondary provider (a config flip, not a redeploy) keeps the feature up, and a graceful fallback covers the overlap. Infinite retries just pile on cost and latency, and doing nothing leaves customers with errors.

prompt: TRADE-OFF: When the daily spend on an AI feature crosses your hard cap, you can either hard-fail requests (return an error) or degrade gracefully (serve a cheaper-model or cached answer). What is the trade-off between these two choices?
choices: There is no trade-off; hard-failing is always correct because it saves the most money
Hard-failing strictly bounds cost and is simplest, but breaks the user experience; graceful degradation preserves a usable (lower-quality) answer at some extra cost and complexity
Graceful degradation is always free and always higher quality, so it has no downside
Both options produce identical user experience, so the choice only affects logging
explanation: It is a real trade-off: hard-failing gives the tightest cost ceiling and is trivial to build but shows users an error; graceful degradation keeps the feature usable with a cheaper or cached answer, at the price of some extra spend and added complexity. The right pick depends on how critical the feature is to the user.
